title: Genesis 21
subtitle: The Birth of Isaac; Hagar Expelled; Treaty at Beersheba

notes.note: MacArthur: 'The LORD was gracious to Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did for Sarah what he had promised' -- the double emphasis on the word/promise of the LORD marks Isaac's birth as the fulfilment of a divine commitment, not the result of natural processes. Sarah's conception at 90 is a miracle of sovereign grace.

MacArthur: Sarah's laughter (v.6) is now the laughter of fulfilled joy rather than incredulous doubt (18:12). 'Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children?' -- the rhetorical question captures the utter unexpectedness of what God has done. The impossible has happened; the covenant promise is now a nursing child.

heb.gloss: to visit / attend to / remember and act
heb.paragraph: "Now the LORD was gracious to (pāqad) Sarah as he had said" (v.1) — the verb pāqad carries covenantal force beyond mere attention: it is purposive, transformative divine action. God "visited" Sarah and her barrenness was reversed. The same verb marks the beginning of the Exodus: "I have indeed pāqad you" (Exod 3:16). Divine visitation is always oriented toward covenant fulfilment — God comes to bring about what has been promised.

"God has brought me laughter (śĕḥōq), and everyone who hears about this will laugh with me" (v.6) — Sarah's exclamation turns Abraham's overwhelmed laughter (17:17) and her own nervous laughter (18:12) into the name of the covenant child. Yiṣḥāq carries all three reactions into one: the laughter of impossibility overcome, of divine promise fulfilled beyond expectation, and of shared communal joy. The child's very name announces that God answers with joy what seemed impossible to human assessment.

Abraham "made a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned" (v.8). The feast (mišteh — drinking/feast) marks the child's survival through infancy — a significant threshold in the ancient world when child mortality was high. The "great feast" signals the covenant child's status: his weaning is a public covenant celebration. Every subsequent feast in Israel — Passover, Sukkot, the temple dedications — echoes this pattern: covenant milestone marked by communal eating in God's presence.

refs.note: Abraham laughed when Isaac was first promised — now that laughter is answered with laughter of fulfillment.
Sarah's faith enabled conception — "she considered him faithful who had promised."
Paul: "Now you, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise" — Christians share Isaac's identity as miracle children of divine fulfillment.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The birth of Isaac is the fulfilment of the most repeated promise in the Abraham narrative. Sarna: "'The LORD did for Sarah what he had promised' (v.1) — the emphasis is entirely on divine initiative. The vocabulary deliberately echoes the previous promises: the same verbs (pakad, to visit/act; asah, to do; diber, to speak) that appeared in the promises now appear in the fulfilment. The narrative matches word to word."

Sarah's laughter at Isaac's birth — 'God has brought me laughter' — is the resolution of all the previous laughter. Sarna: "The laughter of incredulity (18:12) is transformed into the laughter of joy (21:6). The same word (tsachaq, laugh) that gave Isaac his name and that Sarah denied having performed now becomes her hymn of praise. The narrative traces a complete arc of unbelief transformed into joy."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The three qualifications — "at the very time God had promised him" — stress the precision of divine fulfilment. Alter: "The birth narrative is extremely sparse — no description of the birth itself, no labour, no midwives. All the weight falls on the theological statement: God did what God said, when God said. The fulfilment is reported as a fact, not dramatised. The drama was in the promise; the fulfilment simply arrives."

Sarah's speech at Isaac's birth reverses her earlier laughing denial. Alter notes the same laughter (tsachaq) she denied in 18:15 she now claims joyfully in 21:6-7 — incredulous laughter has become joyful laughter, and the name of the child encodes both.

The birth of Isaac is the first resurrection in the Bible — not literally, but typologically. Calvin: "The birth of Isaac was, in a manner, a resurrection from the dead (cf. Heb 11:12). The very body of Abraham was already dead with respect to the power of begetting children; the womb of Sarah was also dead. From these two dead bodies God brought forth life."

Calvin: Abraham had been long without a son — now one is given, and he does not delay but circumcises him on the eighth day. The prompt obedience models the covenant response: grace received calls immediately for the covenant sign.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "the LORD visited Sarah" (pakad — to attend to, act on behalf of) observes that pakad is the covenantal term of divine action on behalf of the elect (cf. Gen 50:24-25; Exod 3:16; 4:31). The note connects this to the larger biblical pattern of divine visitation as the trigger for covenant fulfilment.

The NET note on Isaac's name discusses the triple wordplay: laughter at the announcement (18:12-15), the promise of the laughing child (17:19), and Sarah's joyful invitation for all to laugh with her (21:6). The name weaves incredulity, promise, and fulfilment into a single arc.

sections.header: Verses 8–21 — Hagar and Ishmael Sent Away

poi.name: Desert of Beersheba
poi.text: Hagar wanders in the Desert of Beersheba after being expelled. The same semi-arid region where Abraham will make his treaty (v.22-34). The well that appears when her eyes are opened was there before she saw it — providence operates in the same geography as the covenant.

notes.note: MacArthur: Ishmael's mocking (metsacheq -- from the same root as Isaac's name) triggers the crisis. Sarah's demand that Hagar and Ishmael be expelled is harsh but receives divine validation (v.12). Paul interprets the two sons as an allegory of law and grace, bondage and freedom (Gal 4:21-31).

MacArthur: God heard the boy crying -- the specific attention to Ishmael's distress reflects God's care for the child born outside the covenant line. The angel's reassurance, the opened eyes to see the well, the promise of a great nation -- God's covenant with Ishmael (17:20) is honoured even as Isaac receives the primary covenant inheritance.

heb.paragraph: Sarah's insistence — "that slave woman's son will never share in the inheritance with my son Isaac" (v.10) — invokes the firstborn principle. Though Ishmael is older, the covenant birthright (bĕkōrāh) belongs to Isaac as the covenant-line son. The OT repeatedly overturns birth-order in favour of divine election: Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over Reuben, Ephraim over Manasseh. The firstborn principle is not annulled but reinterpreted through covenant calling — God chooses the younger, the unexpected, the one marked by divine promise rather than natural seniority.

"God heard the boy crying (qôl hannaʿar)" (v.17) — the divine response to Ishmael's cry in the wilderness echoes the divine hearing that named him: Yišmāʿēl ("God hears"). Though Ishmael is outside the covenant line, God hears his cry and provides water. The same divine responsiveness to the cry of the vulnerable — outside the formal covenant — will characterise the prophetic ethics: "The LORD your God... defends the cause of the fatherless and the widow, and loves the foreigner residing among you" (Deut 10:17-18).

The Hagar-Ishmael narrative consistently uses ʾĕlōhîm rather than YHWH — the universal divine name rather than the covenant name. This is theologically precise: Ishmael's relationship with God is real but not the same as the Abrahamic covenant relationship. God cares for Ishmael (ʾĕlōhîm hears his cry, v.17; ʾĕlōhîm was with the boy, v.20) but the covenant promise runs through Isaac. The distinction between general divine providence and specific covenant calling runs through the Ishmael-Isaac narrative.

hist.context: The expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael is among the most emotionally painful narratives in Genesis. Sarah's demand is harsh; Abraham is "very displeased" (v.11). Yet God endorses it (v.12) and personally cares for Hagar in the wilderness — opening her eyes to a well. The episode repeats the structure of ch.16: human rejection, divine encounter, divine provision. God's care for Ishmael does not diminish but is expressed differently from his care for Isaac.

refs.note: Paul quotes Sarah's words ("Cast out the slave woman") as a metaphor for the obsolescence of the Mosaic covenant in relation to the new covenant freedom.
Hagar's second divine encounter parallels the first — God sees and hears Ishmael in the wilderness, repeating the "El Roi" (God who sees) dynamic of ch.16.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ishmael "mocking" (metsachaq — piel of tsachaq) at the feast celebrating Isaac's weaning. Sarna: "The verb is significant: Ishmael uses a form of the same root that gives Isaac his name. The 'laughter-son' is being laughed at by the one whose name means 'God hears.' Sarah's response — 'get rid of that slave woman and her son' — is harsh, but Paul's reading (Gal 4:29-30) gives it theological grounding: the bond woman and her son cannot share the inheritance with the free woman's son."

God hears Ishmael's cry — the name Ishmael means 'God hears' — and provides the well. Sarna: "The second Hagar narrative (ch.21) parallels the first (ch.16): Hagar in the wilderness, divine messenger, promise for Ishmael, provision of water. The pattern is deliberate. God's care for Ishmael does not diminish the covenant priority of Isaac; both lines receive divine attention."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Hagar places the child under a shrub and sits at a distance, "for she thought 'I cannot watch the boy die.'" Alter: "The maternal grief is unbearable to witness — the mother who cannot watch her child die, placing him at arm's length so she will not have to see the moment. The scene is one of the most emotionally devastating in the entire Abraham narrative."

The divine voice calling to Hagar echoes the divine call to Abraham in moments of crisis. Alter notes that Hagar — an Egyptian slave — is the recipient of a direct divine appearance and a covenant-style promise ('I will make him into a great nation'). The covenant community's boundaries are more porous than its internal narratives suggest.

Sarah's demand is harsh, but Calvin defends it: "Although Sarah was carried beyond due bounds by the heat of her anger, yet the substance of the demand was approved by God. The casting out of the bondwoman and her son was necessary for the preservation of the covenant line." The harshness of the means does not negate the validity of the distinction being maintained.

Calvin: The well was there all along — Hagar could not see it. God opens her eyes to what had been present but unseen. This teaches that our extremity is not the absence of divine provision but our own blindness to it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on metsachaq (mocking/playing) observes this is a piel stem of the same root tsachaq that gives Isaac his name. The note surveys the range of meaning: laughing at, playing, mocking. The note acknowledges the ambiguity — the action may have been mere play that Sarah interpreted as mockery, or genuine derision. Paul's use in Gal 4:29 reads it as persecution.

The NET note on the water running out discusses the survival timeline: perhaps two or three days without water in the desert. The theology: both Hagar's cry (v.16) and Ishmael's cry (v.17) are heard — the divine response addresses the child's need first, consistent with the promise to his mother in ch.16.

poi.name: Beersheba
poi.text: Beersheba means either "well of seven" or "well of the oath" — the etymology is preserved in both directions. Abraham plants a tamarisk tree here and calls on the name of the LORD: Beersheba becomes a covenant worship site. It will be the southern anchor of the land ("from Dan to Beersheba").

tl.name: Treaty at Beersheba — this passage
tl.text: Abraham plants a tamarisk tree at Beersheba and calls on the name of the LORD. "Abraham stayed in the land of the Philistines for a long time."

notes.note: MacArthur: Abimelech's observation -- 'God is with you in everything you do' -- is the pagan king's acknowledgment of the covenant presence. The treaty Abraham makes with Abimelech is the first recorded covenant between the covenant people and a Gentile nation. The covenant man becomes a treaty partner with the nations around him.

MacArthur: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba and called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God (El Olam). The divine name El Olam is used here for the first time -- God's eternal, unchanging character is the theological foundation on which the covenant oaths are made. Covenant-keeping requires an eternal God who cannot change.

heb.gloss: Beer-sheba — well of seven / well of oath
heb.paragraph: The place-name Beersheba (v.31) is a pun with two etymological possibilities: bĕʾēr šebaʿ can mean "well of the oath" (šābûaʿ) or "well of seven" (šebaʿ — the seven ewe lambs set aside as witnesses). The name encodes the covenant legal action: a treaty was concluded, confirmed by seven lambs, and witnessed by a well. Place-names in Genesis are frequently covenant markers — the landscape is inscribed with the history of divine-human agreements.

heb.gloss: the Eternal God / God of Eternity
heb.paragraph: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree and "called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God (ʾēl ʿôlām)" (v.33). The title ʾēl ʿôlām — unique here in the Pentateuch — grounds the covenant in divine eternality. The tamarisk tree (a long-lived tree in a semi-arid landscape) is a living monument to the eternal God who guarantees the treaty. The combination of physical monument (tree) and divine title (ʾēl ʿôlām) establishes that covenant agreements with the eternal God transcend the political arrangements of any particular generation.

Abraham "lived in the land of the Philistines for a long time" (v.34) as a ger — a resident alien without full legal rights. The patriarchal status is consistently one of sojourning in the promised land without yet possessing it. Hebrews 11:9-10 celebrates this: Abraham "was living in the promised land like a stranger in a foreign country... he was looking forward to the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God." The sojourner status is not a failure of the covenant but its present mode — the tension between promise and fulfilment is built into the covenant's temporal structure.

hist.context: The expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael is among the most emotionally painful narratives in Genesis. Sarah's demand is harsh; Abraham is "very displeased" (v.11). Yet God endorses it (v.12) and personally cares for Hagar in the wilderness — opening her eyes to a well. The episode repeats the structure of ch.16: human rejection, divine encounter, divine provision. God's care for Ishmael does not diminish but is expressed differently from his care for Isaac.

refs.note: Paul quotes Sarah's words ("Cast out the slave woman") as a metaphor for the obsolescence of the Mosaic covenant in relation to the new covenant freedom.
Hagar's second divine encounter parallels the first — God sees and hears Ishmael in the wilderness, repeating the "El Roi" (God who sees) dynamic of ch.16.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The treaty at Beersheba with Abimelech is Abraham's first formal legal agreement with the inhabitants of Canaan. Sarna: "The treaty establishes Abraham's legal standing in the land he has been promised but does not yet possess. He is recognised by the regional king as a man of God's favour. The well — the subject of the treaty — becomes the site of worship (v.33) and the place from which Abraham will operate for some time."

Abraham plants a tamarisk tree and calls on "the LORD, the Eternal God" (El Olam). Sarna: "El Olam — the Eternal God — appears only here and in Isaiah 40:28. The name connects the eternal nature of God to the eternal covenant promise. Abraham plants a tree that will outlive him, in a land he does not yet own, and calls on the God of eternity."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Abimelech says to Abraham: "God is with you in everything you do." Alter: "The pagan king's testimony is the clearest possible external validation of divine favour on Abraham. Throughout the Abraham narrative, Abraham's character is often compromised; but the divine favour that surrounds him is consistently visible to outsiders. The covenant blessings have made Abraham recognizable as a man of God even to those outside the covenant."

Beersheba's etymology carries two meanings simultaneously — 'well of seven' (sheva) or 'well of oath' (shvua). Alter notes biblical place-name etymologies typically work at multiple levels without resolving the ambiguity; both meanings are active.

The planting of the tamarisk tree and calling on El Olam: Calvin: "Abraham, having obtained the possession of a well by lawful agreement, offers thanks to God, and calls upon him by a new name. He consecrates this place to the service of God, that it may remain as a monument that God had kept his promise. The eternal God is invoked precisely at the point where the eternal covenant is finding its first geographical foothold in Canaan."

Calvin: Abraham planted a tree that would endure long after him — the act of a man who believes the promise. He plants for his descendants. The tamarisk tree is the physical sign of faith in the covenant's long-term reality.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on El Olam ("the Eternal God" or "the Everlasting God") notes this divine title appears only here in Genesis and connects to Isaiah 40:28. The note discusses whether "eternal" refers to God's existence (without beginning or end) or to his covenant faithfulness (everlasting in its commitments). The note favours the latter in this context — Abraham invokes God as the one whose promises are eternally reliable.

The NET note on the Beersheba treaty identifies the formal elements: grievance stated, acknowledgment made, witnesses invoked, oath sworn, gifts exchanged. The ancient Near Eastern treaty form is present in condensed form.

ppl.name: Isaac
ppl.role: Son of Abraham and Sarah; covenant heir
ppl.text: Born at the exact moment God promised. His name embodies the laughter of fulfilled promise. He will be the "only son" whom Abraham will be asked to sacrifice (ch.22), the covenant heir through whom all the promises flow.

ppl.name: Hagar
ppl.text: Again in the wilderness, again heard by God, again given a promise for her son. This second encounter confirms that Genesis 16 was not an anomaly — God's care for Hagar and Ishmael is consistent and personal.

ppl.name: Ishmael
ppl.role: Son of Abraham; expelled but blessed
ppl.text: Heard by God in the wilderness ("God heard" = the meaning of his name, vindicated). He will become "a great nation" (v.18). Later he and Isaac will bury their father together (25:9) — a poignant image of the half-brothers' relationship.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Now the LORD was gracious to Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did for Sarah what he had promised."ESV"The LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did to Sarah as he had promised."KJV"And the LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did unto Sarah as he had spoken."NASB"Then the LORD took note of Sarah as He had said, and the LORD did for Sarah as He had promised."

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls — Genesis Apocryphon
src.quote: The Aramaic paraphrase of Genesis expands on Isaac's birth with additional detail about the joy of the occasion and the fulfillment of the divine promise.

rec.text: Chrysostom celebrated Isaac's birth as a lesson in patience: God waited until all natural hope was gone so that the miracle would be unmistakable. The delay was pedagogical — teaching Abraham and Sarah to trust in divine power, not human biology.

Paul's allegory reads Hagar/Ishmael as Sinai/law and Sarah/Isaac as Jerusalem above/Spirit. The expulsion becomes a metaphor for the supersession of law by gospel. The reading has been criticized for its effect on Hagar's reputation, but Paul's concern is covenant typology, not Hagar's character.

rows.label: Isaac: Incredulity Becomes Delight
rows.text: Sarah's laughter at ninety -- ridicule that becomes delight. The three-part wordplay (tsachaq) runs from incredulity (18:12) through the promised name (17:19) to joyful laughter (21:6). The name Isaac encodes the whole journey.

rows.label: The Well Appears: Providence Pre-Exists the Crisis
rows.text: Hagar's eyes are opened to a well that was already there. God's provision pre-exists the crisis; prayer opens eyes to receive what grace has already placed. The water existed before the prayer.

hebtext.gloss: to visit / attend to / remember and act
hebtext.note: "Now the LORD was gracious to (pāqad) Sarah as he had said" (v.1) — the verb pāqad carries covenantal force beyond mere attention: it is purposive, transformative divine action. God "visited" Sarah and her barrenness was reversed. The same verb marks the beginning of the Exodus: "I have indeed pāqad you" (Exod 3:16). tx.title: Ishmael 'playing' — mĕṣaḥēq
tx.content: MT reads that Sarah saw Ishmael mĕṣaḥēq ('laughing/playing'). The LXX adds 'with her son Isaac' (μετὰ Ισαακ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς), making the conflict interpersonal. Targum Onqelos renders it as 'sporting,' while some rabbinic traditions interpret it as idolatry, sexual immorality, or mock-worship.
tx.note: The root ṣ-ḥ-q puns on Isaac's name. The LXX expansion and rabbinic elaborations all attempt to justify Sarah's extreme reaction, which the MT leaves unexplained.

tx.title: Tamarisk tree at Beersheba
tx.content: MT reads ʾēšel ('tamarisk tree'). LXX translates as ἄρουραν ('field/cultivated land'), apparently reading ʾăšēlāh or confusing the word. Targum Onqelos renders it as 'orchard' (pardēs). The three witnesses give three different pictures of Abraham's activity at Beersheba.
tx.note: The LXX's 'field' reading transforms a tree-planting into agriculture, possibly avoiding association with sacred tree worship. The divergence illustrates how a single rare Hebrew word can fracture across the versions.

debate.title: Hagar's Expulsion: Moral Problem or Providential Design?

positions.name: Moral problem
positions.argument: The expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael is ethnic cleansing motivated by Sarah's jealousy. Abraham's compliance is morally culpable. The text records without approving.

positions.name: Providential design
positions.argument: God explicitly authorises the expulsion (21:12: "listen to whatever Sarah tells you") and provides for Hagar in the wilderness (21:17-19). The separation is not cruelty but covenant necessity — and God cares for both lines.

coaching.tip: Sarah says 'God has brought me laughter, and everyone who hears about this will laugh with me' (v. 6). The Hebrew wordplay is dense: Isaac's name (yitzhak) turns Sarah's earlier laugh of disbelief (18:12) into communal joy. The promise-fulfillment arc transforms the same emotion from mockery to worship.

Hagar and Ishmael's expulsion parallels their earlier flight (ch. 16) — wilderness, despair, divine intervention, a well. But this time God's promise to Hagar is national: 'I will make him into a great nation.' Two covenant lines emerge from Abraham's household, and God cares for both.

prayer_prompt: As you consider Sarah's laughter turning from disbelief to joy at Isaac's birth, consider asking God whether there is a promise in your life that you have stopped expecting Him to fulfill — and whether He is about to surprise you with laughter.
